event: Early hominins inhabit the region, leaving stone tools in East Africa's Rift Valley among the oldest evidence of human ancestors.
Anatomically modern humans, living as hunter-gatherers, occupy areas around Lake Victoria and the region's forests.
Bantu-speaking agriculturalists begin migrating into the region, introducing farming, ironworking, and new social organization.
Early iron-smelting communities flourish around Lake Victoria, producing high-temperature furnaces and carbon-steel tools.
Bantu farming societies consolidate, while Nilotic and Luo-speaking pastoralists move into the northern and central regions.
The Empire of Kitara (the Chwezi/Bachwezi dynasty) emerges as a powerful state associated with the Bigo earthworks.
The Bunyoro-Kitara Kingdom rises under the Babiito dynasty, becoming the dominant power in the interlacustrine region.
The Kingdom of Buganda begins expanding along the northwestern shores of Lake Victoria, challenging Bunyoro's supremacy.
The kingdoms of Ankole, Toro precursors, and Busoga chiefdoms develop centralized rule across the southern lands.
Buganda grows into a centralized state under the kabaka, organizing a sophisticated bureaucracy and large standing forces.
Arab and Swahili traders from the coast reach Buganda, opening long-distance trade in ivory, slaves, and firearms.
Kabaka Mutesa I ascends the throne of Buganda, expanding the kingdom's power and engaging foreign traders and missionaries.
British explorer John Hanning Speke reaches Buganda and identifies Lake Victoria as a source of the Nile.
Mutesa I invites Christian missionaries after meeting Henry Morton Stanley, opening the kingdom to European religious influence.
Anglican Church Missionary Society missionaries arrive in Buganda, soon followed by French Catholic White Fathers.
Kabaka Mwanga II succeeds Mutesa I amid growing religious factionalism among Protestants, Catholics, and Muslims.
Mwanga II orders the execution of Christian converts, creating the Uganda Martyrs later venerated by the Catholic Church.
The Anglo-German Heligoland Treaty places the region within the British sphere of influence.
Britain declares a protectorate over Buganda, beginning formal colonial control of the territory.
The Buganda Agreement grants the kingdom special autonomy, land tenure reforms, and a privileged status within the protectorate.
Construction of the Uganda Railway reaches Lake Victoria, integrating the territory into colonial trade and cash-crop economies.
Cotton becomes a major cash crop, transforming the colonial economy and African smallholder agriculture.
A Legislative Council is established for the protectorate, initially without African representation.
Britain deposes and exiles Kabaka Mutesa II after a dispute over federation, triggering a major political crisis in Buganda.
Mutesa II returns from exile as a constitutional monarch following a negotiated settlement with the British.
Uganda gains independence from Britain on 9 October, with Milton Obote as prime minister and the country joining the United Nations.
Uganda becomes a republic with Kabaka Mutesa II as the first ceremonial president and Obote as executive prime minister.
Obote suspends the constitution, attacks the kabaka's palace, and forces Mutesa II into exile, abolishing the kingdoms a year later.
Idi Amin seizes power in a military coup, beginning a brutal eight-year dictatorship marked by mass killings.
Amin expels Uganda's Asian community, devastating the economy by removing tens of thousands of skilled traders and professionals.
Israeli commandos raid Entebbe Airport to rescue hostages from a hijacked airliner, humiliating Amin's regime.
Amin invades Tanzania's Kagera region, provoking a war that would lead to his downfall.
Tanzanian forces and Ugandan exiles overthrow Idi Amin, ending his regime and forcing him into exile.
Milton Obote returns to power in disputed elections, beginning his second presidency amid renewed violence.
Yoweri Museveni launches the National Resistance Army's guerrilla 'Bush War' against the Obote government.
A military coup ousts Obote for a second time, installing a short-lived military council.
Yoweri Museveni's National Resistance Army captures Kampala, and he becomes president, beginning decades-long rule.
The Lord's Resistance Army insurgency begins in northern Uganda, launching a long and devastating conflict.
Museveni's government restores traditional kingdoms, allowing the reinstatement of the kabaka of Buganda as a cultural leader.
Uganda adopts a new constitution establishing a 'no-party' Movement system of governance.
Museveni wins Uganda's first direct presidential election under the Movement system.
A referendum confirms the no-party Movement system, while an Ebola outbreak in Gulu kills hundreds.
Voters approve a return to multiparty politics, but a constitutional amendment also removes presidential term limits.
Museveni wins multiparty elections, and a ceasefire pushes the Lord's Resistance Army out of northern Uganda.
Al-Shabaab bombings in Kampala kill dozens during the World Cup final, retaliation for Uganda's role in Somalia.
Uganda enacts a harsh anti-homosexuality law, drawing international condemnation before courts annul it on a technicality.
Parliament removes the presidential age limit, clearing the way for Museveni to extend his decades in power.
Museveni wins a sixth term in elections marred by a crackdown on opposition leader Bobi Wine and his supporters.
Uganda enacts a new Anti-Homosexuality Act carrying some of the world's harshest penalties, prompting Western sanctions and aid cuts.
Uganda advances its Lake Albert oil project and East African Crude Oil Pipeline, aiming to become an oil producer.
